turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear argwnents next in Tate against Short, Mr. Dorsen, you may procedd when- ever you •·re ready. ARGJMENT OF NORMAN DORSEN, ESQ.

turns.name: ARGJMENT OF NORMAN DORSEN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. This case, arising in Texas, is a sequel to the de- Morris v. Schoonfield. It involves tJ-,e imprisonment of an indi- gent fo1: inability to pay certain fines imposed for traf.fic offenses:. Tie facts are not in dispute. Petitioner, Mr. Tate, committed several traffic offenses in 1966. These offenses in- eluded driving without an operators license, driving a car with expired license plates and going through a red light. Neither alone, nor taken together, \lere these offenses punishable by a jail sentence. They were punishable--- without an operators license? A I think there were three of those. Q Any indication why he didn't go down and get a license? A No, there's nothing in the record on that, Q How much do they cost in Texas? so, Two dollars, I'm sorry, Q Two dollars. A Neither alone, as I said, or taken together, were these offenses punishabl e by a jail sentence. Petitioner Corporation Court. A court whose juri~diction is limited by statute, to offenses punishable by fine alone. He ~,as convioted on the two offe,1ses and fined $75. But the judgement was not executed. This was explained by Pe- titioner at the subsequent h.,beas corpus hearing on the ground that he had paid a lawyer to appeal the oonvictions. In fact the iawyer failed to perfect the appeal. If the appeal had been perfected, the Petitioner would have been entitled to a trial de novo, in the County court, and relieved, at. leaiit templDrarily, from the obligation to pay the fines. ets until August 7, 1968. He appeared then at the same Cor- poration Court. The Court with jurisdiction only t.o punish by fines. And he pleaded guilty to the other charges. The judge imposed fines, which, when added to those outstanding f.rom 1966 totaled $425. Petitioner, being indigent, was unable to pay the fine. Pursuant to Texas statutes, and the Hou£.ton Code, he was dispatched forth,dth to the Houston Prison Farm to serve 85 days in jail, or one day for each $5 of the fine at the statutory rate in Texas, Relevant statutes are found on page 3 of our brief. Now this was on August 7, 1968, on August 28, 1968, Petitioner commenced a ~abeas corpus action and was released the same day on a bond posted by a friend. He had served 21 days and the unpaid fine was now $321 which would subject him to 64 additional days in jail. Q That same friend didn't help him out with the fines? No, he did not, Nr. Justice Black. The hear inc• on the habeas corpus petition was held ir, the county Criminal Court on August 30, excerpts from the record of which appear mn the back of Petitioner.s brief, not in the Lrown appendix. The Certiorari Petition contains the entire record of the habeas corpus hearing. The County Crim5.nal Court denied the habeas corpus petition and the Texas Court ofCriminal Appeals affirmed and the case then cama here on Certiorari. There's one further important factor or point I'd like to make at the outset. Although the State, in its brief seems to dispute the fact that Petittmner was indigent as of August, 1968, when he was sent to jail for nonpayment of a fine, it is, in fact, perfectly clear f.rom the record that the State had stipulated, during the hearing and the habeas corpus petition, has been for all periods therein, and probably always will be." This ls in the Appendix brief, page 7A. Q Oid he own a car? A That does not appear in the record. The reason that that was not explored, Mr. Justice Marshall, is that the guarnishment provisions in the law of Texas exempt levying on the car of a married man, so that car couldn't have been used to pay--- Q Well, he could have sold it- A Well, he might have done that. Q Well, what happens if a man has 64 charges of driving without a license, reckless driving, and doing bodily harm with an automobile? l\nd he's broke? if he has no money, the court, the lower court, the criminal court as this Court has said in the Williams case last year, there are several alternatives. One is, if he has the possibility of earning some money, to stretch out the fine over a period of time. If that won't work, another alternative is to find a job for him, or put him to work on a prison farm, or some- thing of the so~t, but not to confine him, 24 hours a day. Q Working on a prison farm isn't confining him? A Well, what I had in mind there is the ABA, in its minimum standards of sentencing, provides, they call that partial confinement. The study by the ABA concluded that in the case of the fine--- Q Well, I'm sure Texas doesn't have one. A No, they do not. They do not. Q Would that be constitutionally any less offensive if he wer~ tJnly confined 12 hours a day insteaa of 24? A Well, if he 1-1ere confined only for the purpose of having h.Lm worl< out on a job the fine, we suggest it would not be constitutionally offensive. in other words, if the man cannot pay, if it's clear Q Wouldn't that be something at least resembling involunta,:-y servitude if the sentence of the court was that he spend 12 hours a day at the local prison farm, at least 8 hours of which would be spent working at $2 an hour? A I don• t think i-i; would be because, of course, persons convicted of a crime, but even more important than that, we are not claiming, ., wish to be perfectly clear, we are not claiming that the state is powerless to get at the kind of person l:hat Mr. Just.ice Marshalls question refered to. We say that the state can take certain action. The firs~ thing that they can try to do is use the insallment method. if that doesn't work, if there's no liklihood that the irstall- ment method would work, t:ne kind of c1rrangements for working off the fine over a period of time might be an alternative. Now the ABA, the co!11lllission to study the Federal Penal Code, all examine this extremely difficult problem. Because on the one hand, as I've indicated the state should not be po17erless to proceed against the individual. On the other hand, the underlying principle of the William,;, case, as I understand it, is that for the person who is without funds to put him into jail immediately, which is what happeneo here, there was no hearing, there was no exploration of the possibility of using these other. techniques. We say here that in a situation like this, theCourt should deal carefully, to use the phrase that Mr. Justice White used in his concurring opinion in the Morris case, with a man who is without funds, not exempt him, because as I think the Chief Just~ces opinion pointed out there could be a reverse discrimination in the case of a person who's indigent who won't be able to be proceeded against in any ~ay at all. We are not claiming that. We 're saying that if a man has, as this man had, fines totalin9 $425, or whatever the sum would be, that the lower Court must deal with that parson individually. the ABA and by the commiss:!.on to refonn the criminal law, and deal with him in terms of his particular situation. It's possible in this case for example, since he was getting a vererans' check of, I think, of $104 a month, that he would have been able to pay out the fine, say $5 a week, or something of the sort. The infinni ty in the procedurl? he.:e, we• re suggesting, is the immediate transferrence of an individual who is poor, to jail, ~,ithout: exploration of the various altermative methods A Pardon me? Q The $425 fugure. I would assume if he got $100 a month he could have paid the $2 fine. A Well aparently---he had a wife and two children, Q Well, for example, he could have bought a little less gasoline, and paid the $2 fine. A Well--- Q And I could sug~est some other things he could have done. I--- Q I'd --- it with gasoline. A WeJ.1--- Q But I mean a $2 fine is different from a $100 fine. this record, Mr. Justice Marshall, which is from another point and that is explore the very questions that you.•re raising. What we' re saying is that the lo\rer courts in these situations cannot just take a person who hasn't got the funds and put him in jail. Period. We·re not disputing the implications of your questions as I understand them, Hr. Justice. We're saying that as the funds. That does not mean he should be exempt . I want to em- phasize that again and again, because I think that's a critical just take him and put him in jail. That more is required by the: Constitution to fulfill the mandates of the case as cited in Williams and the philosopgy of the Williams case itself as I read it. Q And that more you say, Mr. Dorsen, could be met by Texas if it showed upon investigation, find that this man is not ab-1.e, to be sure, to pay the whole $'.125 now, but that he can reasonably afford to pay $10 a month, because he does get it--- A That's correct. Q At the very least $104 a month from the--- 0 That's correct, sir. Q The Ve~erans Association. A That is correct, sir. Q Whet:her or not he's employed, and--- A That is correct, sir. Q So then they order him to do that. Then he aoesn' pay the $10 a month, can they then put him in jail? A Well, we would say in a case like that, that the court should then hold the kind of informal hearing--- he can afford to pay $10 a month, out of $104 ~month.Then he doesn't pay it. Then can they put him in jail? A I would say th,1t' s a case of wilfull refusal to clear that--- change the facts a little bit. I suppose if the staaute were you be here then, rich or poor? A If the fine were onty $10? Q No, if there we,:e no fine. If the statute were 10 days in jail for this offense. The statute or ordinance. A I assume we would not be. Q You would not be here? I guess my question then is, if you were to prevaiJ. here, in this lawsuit, would not the resulting tendency in ordinances and statutes be to make all these offenses punishable only by imprisonmnet? A There might be such a tendency. It's hard to speculate about what would happen in each state, the fact of the matter is, I think, Mr. Justice Blak:kmun, 1:hat many states now and some of those, I understand, have not changed the law re- garding to sentencing, but I would like to emphasize one other point which is related to your question. The state makes much of the point, and I think per- haps one or two of the questions that have been asked indicate a concern aboug it, but this is a scoff-law. That this is a fel· would feel are binding in connection with operating a motor ve- hicle, or a license. can provide for jail ~entences, in cases of this kind. Repeat- state of Texas has not chosen to do that. The second point, but we are emphasizing that that wou~d be a perfectly valid mode of corrections! philosophy. The second point we would emphasize on this problem, to use are going to go to jail, for being scoff-laws, rich people should go to jail as well a; poor people. of the state of Texas, and I assume there have been some people who have done that, in Texas as well as in New York and in other states, we say that the laws should be applied equally, to the r ich and the poor. Texas could decide to put them in jail. We are not at all disputing that power. In this connection--- Q What you're suggesting here, is that if you pre- vail here, you may provide an impetus for just that thing, and of course it ties in with the thinking of some people that one of the solutions to our traffic carnage is to impose stiffer--- A That may well be. Q ---regulations. A I would not dispute that, is in the original question. I might say, in connection with theterms of the states interest, the state at several points, and very understandably from its vJewpoint, suggests that this would be terrifically inconvenient for the state to do. Terrifically inc~nvenient to do the kinds of things that the ~.BA has suggested, the~ the commisison to reform the federal law, fh~ federal criminal code has suggested, that Maryland new does, that Delaware does, that California does. Now we sa~ that that is, that the inconvenience problem is, to use the vernacular, a red herring. That all the state already has a probation apparatus, it already has people who are concerned with sentencing, the statutes of the state of Texa& provide, incidentally, installment payments of fines above $200 in another connection. If you are fined above $200 and as a condition for to provide for installments, that the state doesn't find that inconvenient. And the American Bar Association which went through all of this at great care, concluded that it is not too much to ask, in the interest of equal protection, to treat the people without funds a little bit more carefully. I might say, I passed the point by, I suppose it might have been implied in what I said, that this case is really like that can be provided in offenses of this kind is zero days. Which brings it within ·che opinion of the Chief Justice and the concurring op:~nion on the dua process theory by Mr. Justice Harlan. The amicus curaie, I might say, the National Legal Williams, a more severe deprevation of constitutional rights, because here you're not just adding to an existing jail term whic:~ is substitutingjjail for a pecuniary penalty. dollars? Q Per offense. A That's right. So $1800 presumably would have been the ab&olute jurisdictional limit, but they may have --- their limits --- that. Now there is another aspect. to this case. It's not a princi~al point, but we would like to stress it. It is thG idea that exchanging a man's freedoa for $5 a day, strikes us itself as being implicitly irrational. class, or with comfortable means, or just not an indigent, would feel about his freedom, and to translate that precious thing, it's on page 12 of our brief, that, page 16, 1•~ sorry, page ssibility of a brutally long sentence, and provides as a meas- which bearc; no relation to the factors which ought to govern the choice as to the length of the sente11ce. • Q When was this statute --- in Texcs? is it some statute •rem back· - A Well, i t ' s a rather old statute,I believe. Texas law a hundred years ago, in 1850 had a statute which pro- v i ded for 5.nstallment of some kind, but was repealed---the state will correct me on that. It• .i not a new statute in the state of Texz.s. Q But I suppose you'd agree in terms of the econ- omic argument you're making that ~t the turn of the century $5 a day might not have been irrational,--- A Well, that's corr'9ct. We're not making a moral judgement on the legislature of Texas in enacting this claim. I think perhaps notions of fairness have changed as well as a decline in the value of a dcllar . And it seems to me, that one court, an Ohio court incidentally, held, we cite the case in that long ·footnote page 16, that $3 was irrational , we would say that $5 a day Q Those would be the same if it was a thousand A That's right. I'm just emphasizing this other aspect which is a separate point, and is not, as you point out our principal point. put on a state prison farm and required to work B hours a day at a $2 an hour r,te, or something, perhaps something geared to the minimum wages under the Federal Act--- construction job, or any other wo:ck under compelled statecsuper- vision. A I think he could .:>e. I would not think that the state should use that as its first alternative. I think it should follow the recommendations made by the American Bar Association, and the commission, that--- Q You say the state should follow, a;:e you then talking in terms of advice to the state legislatures, how can this Court or ~ny court give advice to the state legis- A I did not mean to put it that way. What I'm It seems to us fully consistent with this Courts opiuions in a variety of cases that the state should use the least res~ric- tive means in enforcing its laws. be someone who was of a special kind, perhaps, but that does not mean that he'& an outlaw, that he should be comdemned tot- ally. He should be given an opportunity to pay off. And he ::ould have, perhaps, nd incidentally this is relevant to our 3econd principal point, and that ~s that the lower courts in some kind of proceeding to find out what's happening in these cases. And it cannot be just mass produced justice and an auto- matic incarceration. If I have any time left, I'd like to just reserve a minute o~ two, if I may, thank you. ARGUMENT OF JOSEPH G. ROLLINS, ESQ.

turns.name: JOSEPH G. ROLLINS
turns.text: Mr. ehief Justice, and May itplease the Court. At the outset, we would like to discuss what this case does not concern. It does not concern any troubles that Preston A. Tate may have had with his lawyers. We think that that is not in the case. Nor docs it involve any arbi~r ary action on the part of the State of Texas in charging two dollars That is not in the case. •rhe case actually boils down tbethe fact that this man, Preston A. Tate, ran a red light in the city of Houston, and the policeman stopped him, and it is part of our routine procedure they asked h im to show his drivers license. He didn't have a irivers license. He went into Cor- poration Court, he plead Not Guilty, the jury fined him $50 on one offense, they could have gone up to $200 . They fined him $25 on the other. That very same day, at 6 :00 p.m. that evening he ran a £"top sigh and fortunat.ely a pol iceman saw him and stopped him and again he was arrested, and then subsequently, he ran up a total of nine traffic offenses. Q Did I understand you to say that Texas found him guilty of two oifenses for not having a drivers license on a single day? A No sir, the day of his trial, the day that the jury found him guilty and fined him a $50 and a $25 fine, that same evening, the offense had occured a week or so earlier, but on the day of h~s trial--- Q ··--and he was coming heme from his trial. something p.m., and I don't believe that he would be coming ran a stop sign and again was given a ticket, he wasn't arrested And that is something else that we would like to point out here, they say that he uas immediately and forthwith taken he rnight have had a wife and chil-:i at the time of the habeas corpus hearing, ne had no children when he committed these of- fenses, in other words there was enough time lag in there for him to have fathered two childrem. Sor. don't think the state of Texas has really been forthwith on this thing. In fact I think perhaps if we were to be critized we could be critized in dragging our heels and flOt bringing this th~ng to a head before now. Anothe~ thing in the Petitioners brief, they question the jurisdiction of our Corporation Court, and r think he an- swered h,.s OW?'l question when he stated that the Corporation Court could put a man in jail after he had failed to pay his installments. Well now, if we can put him in jail after failing to pay hos installments, we could---we would have the jurisdiction beforehand, and besiaes, our Texas Supreme Court and Texas Court ofCrirninal Appeals says the Conporation Courta and the J.P. Cou~ts have the right to enforce their orders by incar- ceration and I don't think that it would serve any purpose to argue that further. Q What effort was made during this year or two to collect the fines? A That, Ur. Justice Marshall, I don't know. Q Well now, at the hearing when he was sent aw3y, what happened there, other than "You don't have the money, so you go." A Yes, sir, that's it,, Q That's all? A Yes. Q Well, \10Uld it have been possible to find out whether he could have paid it in installments? A Well--- Q Obviously the judge could have asked one ques- tion, can you pay this $10 a month, or--- A There is no provision in our law for paying a misdemeanor of this type out in installments. Q Is there anything in the law that prohibits it? A 1--- Q I would assume in this particular case, if the judge had said on the day he was fined $75, you have got two years to pay this $75, there would have been no question about that, you could do that under the Texas law, couldn~b you? A Two years? Q Yes. A I don't believe, Mr. Justice Marshall, that--- Q J:s that. what happened, it was two years before they got around to asking him for it? A It was two years before ~hey---actually what happened here, they had some capiases out on him, on some of these .:,'·her subsequent offenses and they finally arrested him, they finally ran him down. Now whether we didn't run hard enough or not, I don't know. Q Oh, you didn't delibe~ately delay it, I didn't--- A No, sir. In fact he was, as I say, and I was not Q Did he ever open a defense for these cases? A Seven of them were nolo COJ?,tendere, and the two that he plead not guilty on, two the jury found him guilty,--- Q What was his defense? A I don' t kno11, sir, I don't know, but when a per- son gets caught red handed running a red light--- Q ---what his defense was, or has been? · as that he was too poor to pay thefine. 8 To pursue Justice Marshalls question about in- stallment payments, I assume Texas charges interest on its out- .;;tanding fines, doesn't it? A No, sir, we do not. Q So at l~ctst he doesn't have $3 a month interest A No, sir. Q On that ten dollars. A And to continue with Justice Marshalls question, to speak, on how he's going to collect his fines. Q I assume you also make the point that it's ~if- ferent than when he was flrst picked up and convicted,, and got $75 that the judge had said either this or you go to jail. And this case, where by the time they got around to him he had nine other charges against him, I think those are two different cases. Don't you? A As far as equities, yes, but as far as the law is concerned w~ think that---and the man could have---if he had just obeyed the law, right there, none of this trouble would have happened. But he chose to disobey the law, he chose to :!rive without a drivers license for---he admitted on the habeas =orpus 2½ years, and as of last week, he hadn't got a drivers license yet. And we don't think that, of course, as far as ·the it certainly should be something teat we should not be blind to, Q My point is thi'3 same judge does have right of .. ·ton? Court was conderoning in Wjlliams was an instance where they nad a statute wh.ich provided for imprisonment regardless. In addition, in this same statute, 'there was a pro- vi-.ion for a fine. And what this Cou:z:·t said, at least the way I read it, and I believe that's what the concurring opinion in the Schoonover case also said was their interpretation of it, was that this Court said that when you translate a fine into jail tine provided in the statute. That was wha'i:. \las before the Court, they---I believe ~ou all said that you had a very limited question, and that's But I was impressed by the number of times that Mr. ehief Justice Burger, in his opinion, kept referring to that circumscrjption of the case to cases where it extended the total And in this case, this is a case where the only sano- tion for violation of this statute was a fine only. We submit and we think we have shown it in our ~riefs that in cases like this that our other statutes which provide that upon failure to pay a fine you may be laid out in jail at $5 a day, we think these two 5tatutes should ce construed in pari materia, in other words to show that the legislature'intended that the man should not get off scott-free. But, that if he would pay the fine, that's fine, if he couldn't than he would have to lay it out in jail. Q Mr. Rollins, in the Appendix of the opinion of the Court in Williams, last year, there was an effort to sum- I marize what various states did, and as to Texas, of course it didn't purport to be complete, but it was recited that he made the Defendant found guilty and finecbmnay be put to work or im- prisoned for a·suffieient length of time to discharge the am- ount. Is there a provision, a work-out provision under Justice of the Peace fine, or--- A Your Honor, I can answer that for the city of Houston, that we have an ordinance that provides that if he will voluntarily go to what \IC call 011r P farm, he can work that out record, Q Well, that's rather an important aspect in view of what was said in the williama case about alternatives, wouldn would,1' t you til:.i.nk? A Your Honor, the, actually as far as principle is cerati,ng him where he can just lie around in jail and talk to the boys in the cell, or go ou·i.. and work on a prison farm. Its--- Q Well, maybe concievably take a different view of A Yes, sir, that's obvious, sir, but I-- -you asked me the question cold, I can't see that---the principle is that the man is being restrained obviously it would be in- voluntarily. Q Mr . Do~sen certainly didn't argue that there was anytting constitutionally unpermissable, you will recall, and--- A But I couldn't understand--- Q ---·that---naid he would agree with that, but since all of the traatnent of th5.s difficult pfoblem has dealt in t~T.'ltls of alternatives, it's convievable that even at the rate of $1 an hour, which Texas allows, plus presumably some food, on a worl. out day, you might have a different case than the case you have without that evidence on the record. A Oh. That's a city ordinance. they give the man something to eat whan he's doing this work. A Yes, sir, I can say that we feed real good. Q Well, that might be quite a different: case from the one you have here. A Your Honor,· I cannot see the difference. The man- 1,'hat about a chain gang? And leased him ' out to a private road contractor, and say that if you go out here and work on this chain gang, out in the hot sun, well, we'll let you off a lot sooner. I can't see the difference in principle in the idea is that the man is being rest.,ained from getting out in society. cai:.e like we have here, where the legislature has stated that ought. to be construed in pari materia, that if he does not pay the fine, then he has to go to jail. We think t.hat that is entirely different and we would submit, there's two e rly cases out of California which we did not inch.de 1.n out brief but would like to submit to the Court. Pacific 372. Q Could you furnish us with a memorandum of those so I--- would like to pr.int in the Wadley case, in a concurring opinion Mr. Justice McFarland mentioned that there, or classified that there are three types of &anctions for misdemeanor fines: im- prisorur.ent only, fine only, and imprisonment plus fine. In in re Rosenheim, there was a situation very ~im- ilar to the situation that was before you all in Williams. And the Cnurt there, in the California Court, said that the language of the statute had an ambiguity because if the legis- lature had really wan·l:ed or had clearly wanted the fine to be transmuted to jail time and added on to the imprisonment time, they could bave clearly so said. But that they didn't. And that the misdemeanant had strict construction of the statute agains<= the state and for the pr soner. In whichcase, and they further stated that tf the loose construction, that if tte state wanted in that case, were followed, then it wa& possible for a judge to do by in- direction what he could not do directly. And I believe you all used that same phrase in William. Q Mr. Rollins? A Yes, sir. Q How long have you had your P farm orders? A Long before the times germaine here. Q Judicially noticeable? A Your Honor, in Texas, city ordinances are not--- notice of city ordinances, they do of city charters. So in strictly legal point of view, I would presume ~hat a court could not take judicial knowledge of city ordin- ance which sets up--- Q ---is to judicial notice doesn't necessarily go- vern what the law might---as to judicial notice. A Well··-- Q Why don't you supply us with a citation of those ordinances or. copies of them, when you give the other citations- A Yes, sir. Q As the Justice has suggested. A Yes, sir. It is in the city code, and if the Supreme Court can take judicial knowledge of nur city of Hous- tom code well, the problem is solved. Q Even if we can judicially notice those factors, we can't judicially notice that the record in the habeas corpus procedinga shows that he refused to take this alternative. In fact the record is blank on thc--- A No, sir, if it's not in the record I don't think you can. Q Is there any---was that before the Texas Appellati Court, or--- A Your Honor, r. don't know, I don't think it was. Q Well, I have no idea whether a motion to sup- plement the record would be granted or not, but you certainly are free to make such a motion. A Yes, sir, r will certainly do so, And Your Honor, in what little time I have remaining, I would like to protest very vigorously about this idea of making the state of Texas, or the city of Houston, or any other state, kind of adopt, a--- I guess it's going to be something like the American Automo- bile Association has for their subscribers. all just part of the ser-~ice, We don't think that the Fourteenth Amendment by the wildest stretch of the imagination could re- crimes and misdemeanors. Q Well, a.re you referring now i:o the installment plan? A Yes, sir, In other words, that's really what youtre asking is that what he's asking is that the sherrifs and the chiefs of police Just d~t ~p credits with prople like Pres- ton Tate, and let him pay it out ac co much a month, Then how---it's goinq to take the wisdom of Soloroon invented to correlate the mans family size, his personal sen- sitivity, his, the value• of his car which he has commited the crimes with ~nd all of that into a jumble and come out and I would like to leave in parting with the idea that the poor people in Houston, Texas, outnumber the ri~h, they've got the ballot and the suffrage and if they think the way we collect our fines is wrong they can elect city councilmen and state legis- lators to change the law. That the poor people of Texas have not done so, would be indicative that this law and the way we carry it out is not oppresive and is not unworkable. But expecially since the Fourteenth A.>nendrnent did not have the---and I would like also to point out that the same Congress that wrote the Forteenth Ali-.endment was also the same Congress that passed on our eon- the Fourteenth Amendment before we could get back in the Union, that same Congress that wrote the Fourteenth Amendment thought our state constitution was all right. And we submit that this is strictly a matter of local concern, and thatiit would be beyond the wildest stretch of the Houston to set up this installment payment, but, Your Honors, if you do, please tell us exactly how to work it. Because the District Courts are going to be working overtime, each one with a mutually conflicting subjective idea what a pro?er installment should be and proper collection proceedures should be, and I can see more three-man courts being convened all over Texas and all over the nation ofor that matter, trying to deter- mine whether the installment payment met the cost of· living then Your Honor, I wo1.1ld like to add one thing. What about the puople that just won't pay? We oqght to have some right to do that, and we submit that this is where the problem is, with our people and our legislators. Thank you. Q Do you have any law in Texas to provide for for- feiture of automobilies that are used by people constantly? A No, sir. A married mans automobile is not subject to execution. l'le had a populace inspired legislature in the early 1880s that took care of what the Constitution didn't take care,of, and if a married man in ~exas is prudent enough not to have a bank account, he can go free. Q Mr. Dorsen?

turns.name: ARGJMENT OF NORMAN DORSEN
turns.text: 'Zhank you, Mr. Chief Justice. I think first of all that some of the catalogues of alleged harms that my friend from Texas has referred to are really unrealistic and I would just refer tile quote :,:-athe:.: ti.an go into the details cited in our br::.'~f. The Schoonfie,Jd, which is the Maryland case that was back on ;:emand from the Morris and Schoonfield case can work consistently with Williams and with Illinois and witho undue burden on the state. P fa.rm that was referred to, that is r,ot discussed at -all in the record, is not merely what I had first thought what my friend from Texas it referring about, as I understand it from Texas Counsel, that everyone goes to the so-called P farm, but that people who work there, who agree to work, get $7 a day, for working. Now that's not t .he same thing that I was suggesting before. Because what we were sug,;esting before was absent the wilfullness pC"oblem which I certainly want to re-emphasize that the state is not powerless under the Constitution to incar- cerate someone who wilfully refuses to pay the fine, but absent that--- Q This P farm; is that P., an abbreviation for "prison" or is it Pae, where they grow beef? A I think it's the first, the former. That--- we stand on the alternatives as discussed in the Williams case, I want to be very clearaabout that. The installment process is one process, and the work process is another. A twenty four hour a day confinement is not necessary in the absence of wilfullness to deal with this problem. And as I just want to clear up finally, in half a minute, a tirning problem that may not have been completely clear, and And that is that after Hr.Tate was convict!ed and fined $75 in 1966, as far as this record shows, and there was hearing on this point, as far as the record shows he had no way of knowing until 1968 that that money was due. His lawyer had been disbarred, and his lawyer apparently had failed in this case also to perfect the appeal. We think, in conclusion, Your Honors, that--- Q --·-what Mr. Tates occupation is? odd jobs but I'm not sure what his occupation was. Q What kind of car was this, did it show that? A A Chr~•sler, I don't know the year. Q Does it show the model? 8 It doesn't show the model. But I was just going to say in conclusion that we regard this case in the essential principles as indistinguishable from the Williams case, and we ment below. Q Tha_nk you Profes:,or Dorsen, thank you Mr. Rollins . t:he case is submitted.
